*While*, *after*, and, interestingly, *though* seem good candidates here. *During* still is a good runner-up, but *but* seems far less so. This could however be a bias, as using "but" in scientific texts is sometimes considered poor style.

Overall, I suggest to remove *while*, *after*, *though*, and *but*, plus possibly *during* from the current set of continuations. However, again, "to" is a no-fix: only 3% continuation usage is probably too little to ensure its relevance.

One final test I want to make before removing any words is trying to see if we can measure sufficient amounts of usages of those words right after abbreviations (i.e., where the lower-case continuation is preceded by a dot). If there were, we could make an even more informed decision; if not, it probably is impossible to discern the cases where this was an orthographic error from the cases where it actually was used after an abbreviation.

@azmathmoosa that seems like issue relating to your image being float -- I think imgaug requires data being uint8. See for example the following comment in the Readme

>     # All images must have numpy's dtype uint8. Values are expected to be in range 0-255.

It controls the strength of contrast, i.e. higher alpha means more contrast. The augmenter currently changes each pixel in an input image independently to the new value `alpha * (i - 128) + 128`, where `i` is the original pixel value.

This code spawns a new shell and gets the aliases from it.  This is fine for the common case of stuff defined in shell startup scripts, but not for any subsequently defined things.  Unfortunately I don't think it's generally possible for a sub-process to interrogate the parent shell as to the aliases it has (unless they were to be passed in somehow by the `fuck` alias).

Similarly, exported functions can easily be found in the sub-process environment, but this isn't the case for non-exported functions.  Again, the approach of spawning a new shell and seeing what functions it has will probably suffice, although it will miss some things.

Btw.
> If we return "False" (default), then the client always receives data starting at the beginning of the file!!! This results in later beautiful exceptions like "The remote client closed the connection" and things like that, because the client is not receiving correct data.

As I understand it, this is complaint with WebDAV: if a server chooses to not support ranges, it will not add the `Allow-Ranges` header, and a client must handle that.

Sorry, I got busy there for a bit. I think you're right, that it doesn't warrant the change to the Dict class, but I do think it'd be nice to be able to choose which dict base to have addict use. So with that in mind, what do you think about the changes I just pushed (as part of this pull request). It pulls out the Dict class into the AbstractAddict base class which allows us to create a Dict and OrderedDict class by simply extending it like so:

```py
class Dict(AbstractAddict, dict):
    pass


class OrderedDict(AbstractAddict, BaseOrderedDict):
    pass
```

Yeah, the point contains test is quite slow, and without watertight meshes not going to tell you very much. Some ideas:

1) If you have 2 watertight meshes you can use boolean intersection methods of the Trimesh objects (which uses blender or openSCAD as a backend). This is always going to be a bit slow as well, and can give wonky results from precision issues.

2) You probably want an actual collision detection library- FCL is very popular and this python binding looks pretty interesting: https://github.com/jf---/python-fcl

Good idea, and any PR's would be much appreciated!

**Nearest vertex** is easy, you can just query a scipy kdtree on the vertices.:
```
tree = m.kdtree()
pid = tree.query(points)
```
Unfortunately last time I looked in to adding the kdtree to the cache there were memory management issues which led to hard crashes, so the KDtree (and triangles_tree) has to be instantiated when you use it.

**Nearest triangle center** would be easy (`scipy.spatial.cKDTree(mesh.triangles_center)`) but may not be as generally useful as other things.

**Nearest face index and nearest point on surface** would be super useful, but is definitely more work. You might be able to use the nearest method of triangles_tree to avoid having to do a point- triangle query on every single face (which is going to be pretty slow).

I think the first step for the face queries is writing a good point-triangle function. One option is to assemble a corresponding list of triangles and points (with stacking/tiling) and then throw them into a function in trimesh.triangles that doesn't exist yet. That would at least get most of the heavy lifting into a few numpy calls rather than having to loop over anything. A prototype for that function:

```
# for development
from trimesh.triangles import *

def nearest_point(triangles, points, triangles_normals=None):
    '''
    Given a list of corresponding triangles and points, find the nearest point on each triangle to the given point. 

    Arguments
    --------------
    triangles: (n,3,3) float, triangles in space
    points:    (n,3) float, Cartesian points in space that correspond to triangles
    triangles_normals: (n,3) float, unit normal vectors of each triangle. 
                                     if not passed, generated from cross products

    Returns
    --------------
    nearest:  (n,3) float, Cartesian points on triangle
    distance: (n,) float, distance from input point to nearest point on triangle
    '''

    triangles = np.asanyarray(triangles, dtype=np.float64)
    points    = np.asanyarray(points, dtype=np.float64)

    if len(triangles) != len(points):
        raise ValueError('every triangle must have ONE corresponding point!')
    if triangles_normals is None:
        triangles_normals = normals(triangles)

    # do the things
    pass
```

One way you might use triangles_tree to assemble the list of point- triangle queries. There are going to be some edge cases that probably require playing around with num_results (and possibly adding an intersection query to generate a complete triangle candidates list):
```
In [1]: tree = m.triangles_tree()

In [2]: point = np.array([0,0,0], dtype=np.float64)

In [3]: bound_check = np.append(point, point + trimesh.constants.tol.merge)

In [4]: bound_check
Out[4]: array([ 0.,  0.,  0.,  0.,  0.,  0.])

In [5]: list(tree.nearest(bound_check, num_results=1))
Out[5]: [0]

```

This PR seems to allow recv() to read garbage on the line, such as might often happen on a serial link, as xmodem is often used with. For example, a successful block transfer, followed by a bunch of junk, and then possibly a successful block again later, when the line noise disappears. Do you agree this is the problem solution?

More detail about this code change:
We might find a SOH/STX start-of-header block at any time during this garbage, when we do, go ahead and read a packet_size block and check its CRC. If SOH or STX is just part of the line noise, CRC fails, we send the usual retransmission request -- or it might be a legitimate next block, where CRC succeeds, logging "CRC fine".

The code solution is interesting, perhaps abusing, in redefinition of default "retry=16" into "retry=1029" or "retry=132", the purpose of "retry" is how many retransmissions of a full block should be retried, not individual bytes? perhaps we should define new variable for this purpose, retry_find_start begins at (128+5) at beginning of loop, and redefined as (1024+5) when STX is found, and leave the original user-defined "retry=16" variable alone.

All this set, I would really wish for test coverage of this new code, https://coveralls.io/builds/8429874/source?filename=xmodem%2F__init__.py#L498 but I am sympathetic that there exists no other test coverage for failure in recv() -- if we had some, perhaps this bug would already have been addressed!

No really. The idea of the library is to stick to creating and rendering DOT source for visualization, not to provide a generic API for representing graphs (like e.g. NetworkX). Thereby `Graph` and `Digraph` can essentially be simple *append-only* list of strings with DOT code (no objects keeping track of individual nodes, edges, attributes etc.).

Typically, users already have the (information about the) collection of nodes and edges they want to render somewhere inside *their* objects/data structures (separation of concerns, why duplicate?). Wouldn't it be cleaner to make/use you own modifiable node objects that then use `graphviz` for rendering under the hood (composition).

You can also try `pygraphviz` or `pydot`, which AFAIR implement modifiable DOT elements (which makes more sense for them, as they also *parse* DOT code, i.e. do a full round-trip).

>>> from cpppo.server.enip import client

from cpppo.server.enip import client

This is most likely a bug in gettext: you can reproduce the same behavior without using Rosetta, by e.g. 

1. generating the catalog with `python manage.py makemessages -a`
2. translating the code manually
3. running `python manage.py compilemessages`


It seems that gettext incorrectly sets the `python-format` on the message containing the percent sign. Removing the flag from the catalog fixes the issue. This should probably be adressed in gettext.

@IVANOPT PyRestTest supports several ways to achieve what you need here, in order from simplest to most complex:

* Use the "vars" command line argument to supply runtime variables for  the tests, and a templated request body/url/headers
* Use environment variables, and the env_variable or env_string generators to store environment variables to a string, then use that string in a template
* Use a custom generator extension to create data from some source and use that data in templates (see: [extensions docs](https://github.com/svanoort/pyresttest/blob/master/extensions.md) )
* There is a proposal in flight to provide an extension point for pluggable request body generation (see https://github.com/svanoort/pyresttest/issues/115 )

The [advanced guide](https://github.com/svanoort/pyresttest/blob/master/advanced_guide.md) provides information on how to use these.

Checking for a list with result size >1 (validators element):

```yaml
- validators: 
   - compare: {jsonpath_mini: 'my.value', comparator: 'type', expected: 'list' } 
   - extract_test: {jsonpath_mini: 'my.value.0', test: 'exists' }  
```
The extract_test there is asserting that the 1st element in the list exists, so it'll check for >= 1 element. 

I haven't run the above, so there may be minor typos, but it will do what you want.

@justinkterry Sure, the example code is wrong, as [`RAKE.__init__()`](https://github.com/fabianvf/python-rake/blob/master/RAKE/RAKE.py#L144) needs to be passed the `minCharacters`, `maxWords` and `minFrequency` parameters , [`RAKE.run()`](https://github.com/fabianvf/python-rake/blob/master/RAKE/RAKE.py#L155) only accepts a single `text` parameter.

**Wrong**:
```
import RAKE
Rake = RAKE.Rake(RAKE.SmartStopList())
Rake.run(<text>, minCharacters = 1, maxWords = 5, minFrequency = 1)
```

**Correct**:
```
import RAKE
Rake = RAKE.Rake(RAKE.SmartStopList(), minCharacters = 1, maxWords = 5, minFrequency = 1)
Rake.run(<text>)
```

## Serialization
Is it necessary to serialize the collection itself with something else? Seems like `pickle` or `dill` will serialize, then `multiprocessesing` will serialize the result of that. You have any resources to read or examples that would be informative about the advantage of pickling/dilling the collection itself?

## Object model for `seq/pseq`
My plan in implementing different versions of `seq` is to change the code structure a little bit. The primary change would be to make something like a `class ExecutionEngine` which is constructed similar to how `seq` is now. That is perhaps overrided/inherited like `class ParallelExecutionEngine(ExecutionEngine)` to run in parallel. The class would be callable, have static methods like the ones that `seq` has now (eg `open`, `json`...), and `seq` itself would be an instance/singleton of the class. Overall, I think this would be a fairly clean way to keep lots of code in common, but cleanly separate the differences (and will help with the next thing).

## Parallel Stage Barrier
I think that forcing `collections.Iterable` to expand is along the right track, but it would be better to think about it in terms of synchronization/stage barriers. I would look at it like this:

1. All the functions that are embarrassingly parallel, are marked as `PARALLEL`. This would include `map`, `filter`, and such
2. By default, it is assumed that all other functions are not parallelizable at all
3. When lineage is evaluated, look for blocks of `PARALLEL` transformations, and lazy execute the batched functions all together in a single call to `Pool.map` (by lazy execute, que that up as the next `iter` thing, which is executed in parallel all at once when the first value is asked for).
4. As you noted (which I didn't know), the fact that `Pool.map` preserves order makes this even easier.

The advantage of this is that for transformations that can be collapsed into a single operation (sequential maps, or filters, or such), they will be. The second advantage is that it would be easy to add new functions as `PARALLEL`. Finally, this would allow the possibility for things which are parallelizable with work to be marked as something else such as `PARALLEL_BARRIER` so that blocks of `PARALLEL` operations can still be computed in parallel. It would take some work to go through and mark things correctly as `PARALLEL`, but thats fine.

The last thing to note is that doing this would be some proper motivation for having a version of `_` lambda within `PyFunctional` itself since `fn._` doesn't work as intended in parallel. Prior motivation was for convenience, but this is actually a real concern.

One scenario just occurred to me:
- you make releases for various packages (some use setuptool-scm and some don't)
- you use a tool like zest.releaser that automatically runs check-manifest for you
- you want to continue using the same release workflow for all the packages

In this situation you (I'm using the general 'you' here, not you in specifically) don't need check-manifest to catch errors, but you'd like to prevent it aborting the process with false positives.  It may be helpful to define a setup.cfg option to allow check-manifest to skip these projects, or perform a weaker test (just compare git metadata with sdist, in effect verifying that setuptools_scm works like it's supposed to -- and here I'm making assumptions about what setuptools_scm does, since I've nevert actually used it).

Externally the user interface would be `check-manifest --weak`, or, more likely, a `setup.cfg` with

```
[check-manifest]
weak = 1
```

This would skip the "copy to empty temporary directory without git metadata" part, in effect reverting to the behavior of check-manifest before bug #1 was fixed (with the corresponding disadvantages).  Or -- thinking out loud here -- maybe I want to *copy* the VCS metadata too?

This seems fine, though I've got a couple of suggestions that might make this even more user-friendly:

- Can you add a section to the README about this option?
- If one is handling credentials that are so sensitive that they should never be transmitted in the clear, then it would be better to configure your web server to listen on port 443 only. I understand that many people may be using this in environments where that's not an option (e.g. Heroku) but it might be good to mention this in the README.
- A 403 with no error message other than "Forbidden" may not make it clear to API users why their API call is failing. It would be awesome if you could define a new `werkzeug.exceptions.HTTPException` subclass that you could raise in order to provide an error message about the reason for the 403. The Stripe API has a good example of this:
```
$ curl -i http://api.stripe.com
HTTP/1.0 403 Forbidden
Cache-Control: no-cache
Connection: close
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "error": {
    "message" : "The Stripe API is only accessible over HTTPS.  Please see <https://stripe.com/docs> for more information.",
    "type": "invalid_request_error"
  }
}
```

@nwaxiomatic hummm, yes, the problem is you're creating the occurrences in UTC timezone, and then converting them to Pacific, UTC is "constant" so occurrences will happily generate in constants intervals while Pacific time shifts. You need to generate occurrences using the correct timezone so dateutil generates them taking TZ shifts into consideration. In django-scheduler this translates to giving o_start and o_end a proper  `US/Pacific` datetime instead of `UTC` (it probably deserves a proper method param)

```
2016-11-05 02:00:00-07:00
2016-11-06 02:00:00-08:00
2016-11-07 02:00:00-08:00

2016-11-04 09:00:00-07:00
2016-11-05 09:00:00-07:00
2016-11-06 09:00:00-08:00
2016-11-07 09:00:00-08:00

2016-11-04 09:00:00-07:00
2016-11-05 09:00:00-07:00
2016-11-06 09:00:00-08:00
2016-11-07 09:00:00-08:00
```
AFAIK these occurrences are correct. Beware how naive event dates are handled, they are set to the o_start timezone to generate the occurrences. So the last two events are in `US/Pacific`, and the first one had the event start set to `09:00 UTC` which is `02:00 US/Pacific` at that point in time. Also, the first occurrence for the first event will not show up in these example because event was set to start at `02:00 US/Pacific` and I asked for occurrences after `09:00 US/Pacific`.

IMHO this behaviour is correct but is probably better not to mix timezones when dealing with events and occurrences as it gets hairy very fast. Let me know if you think I missed something.

You cant treat occurrences as events because of never-ending events. That would cause your server to fill up instantly! Instead occurrences are calculated at view time and only stored if modified. This means its just an issue with which occurrences are being calculated to be shown in that case.

I think that the better implementation (given the goals of this project) is to expose an "update_attachment" route (which apparently I didn't get around to doing) and then the calling code is responsible for doing "get -> update/create" as appropriate. 

It's not at all unreasonable to think about exposing a better high level set of functions against Confluence which this might fall into but I don't intend to do that at this point. It could either depend on this package or be another implementation inside the package.

Hmmm. That code (your `honor_...` branch of your Tarrasch repo) passes for me on MacOSX. (I uncommented the skip line.) However, it shows an error "No such file or directory", so maybe I am not running the test right via nose. (Tox sets up an environment, which I'm trying to replicate. I'm avoiding tox so I don't have to wait for *every* test to run.)

What is the problem you are seeing? Your fix seems to avoid trapping KI (by trapping Ex instead of BaseEx) and then to avoid `out_queue.put()` on KI, so I don't see how the deadlock can be related to the put which is now skipped. Is KI happening *during* `out_queue.put()`? If so, it seems like your test does not exercise that.

Try this in your test: Put a long sleep (but not too long, else you'll have trouble killing the procs, as I mention below -- maybe 5seconds) in front of queue.get(). Then, run only the failing test. (I have no idea how to do that with tox, but you can do it with `nosetests --no-skip test/worker_test.py:KeyboardInterruptBehaviorTest.test_propagating_with_par_scheduling`. With that, Ctrl-C should still work! But it doesn't. Instead, the sub-processes keep running after nose has quit, another worker dying every few seconds, which is very annoying. This looks like an architectural problem, or at least a mistake in how you are using nose. I don't have time to dive deep into your code. But you could probably ameliorate the problem by using timeouts for all blocking calls, in a loop. (Both put and get, I think.) As a general principle, nothing should block forever.

If the problem is only for users who have disabled GC, maybe use a try/finally somewhere to close the queue in all workers. But that won't solve the problem I've (maybe) found by sleeping ahead of get().

I think you can force the problem to go away by sending a specific signal to sub-procs. SIGKILL, I guess.

Btw, I am skeptical of your `queue_size = 1`, but any problem there is unrelated.

Unfortunately this is not possible with boto3's model, as you suggested. I am not intimately familiar with how Lambda optimizes requests, but you could ask in the [developer forums](https://forums.aws.amazon.com/forum.jspa?forumID=186&start=0) for tips in that direction. I also found [this blog post](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/compute/container-reuse-in-lambda/) that indicates that initialization code can sometimes be re-used. My (non-expert) impression is that if you create your service clients / resources outside the scope of a function, the can be re-used in some situations.

One thing you can do to save startup time in boto3 is to ignore the service resources and just use the client. There is a little bit of extra initialization that needs to be done on a resource on top of the underlying client it creates. Additionally, a resource will marshall responses into objects rather than passing through the raw response. The client is a bit more difficult to use in terms of programmer effort, and in some cases (such as handling paginated responses) it can be considerably more difficult. You can look at our code and the CLI code to see how we do things, but that in itself can be difficult due to our dynamic model.

Aside from that, I can't think of anything beyond the KISS principle. Using the client forces you to be very explicit about what operations you call, so make sure you aren't calling anything you can get for free or cheaper somewhere else. You can get most of the information about an Object in S3, for instance, without having to download the contents.

I've added the playlist edit support in [my branch](https://github.com/BlackLight/mopidy-spotify/commits/feature/playlist_modify) and I've been adding and removing tracks from my playlist quite happily in the last weeks :)

The feature is implemented on top of [my own branch](https://github.com/BlackLight/mopidy-spotify/commits/fix/incompatible_playlists) to fix the web API playlists issue, but since it just implements the `save` method in `playlists.py` (and the `delete` HTTP method on the web client) it should be relatively easy to port it.

Adding and removing tracks to playlists and changing tracks order should all be working now. The only features that are not implemented are:

* Deleting playlists - it's not supported by the Spotify web API: https://github.com/spotify/web-api/issues/555.

* Adding tracks scattered across the playlist (e.g. adding tracks on position 1, 4, 7 and 8). Anyway, there's no MPD client I know about that supports such a feature.

Here I'd try to summary my points and reasons **why I need this** 
1. Following Single Responsibility Principle. Having organized Pipfile makes it more readable and maintainable. Now I've four requirements file. `base.txt`, `test.txt`, `dev.txt`, `production.txt`

production.txt
```
-r base.txt

# WSGI Handler
gunicorn==19.7.1

test.txt
```
# Test dependencies go here.
flake8
fake-factory
factory_boy
tox
pytest
pytest-factoryboy
pytest-cov
pytest-flake8
pytest-faker
pytest-isort
pytest-env
````
dev.txt
````
# Local development dependencies go here
-r base.txt
-r test.txt

pudb
django-pdb
ipython
ipdb

# debug
werkzeug
```
moving to `pipenv` will make me put those either on `dev` or in or `default` where something like `ipython`, `werkzeug` for example is only related to dev not testing.

2. I'm now working with three docker environments (dev, testing, production). For CI, I see it is better to have different sections. This will reduce the docker image installing part of the requirements not all.

I agree with you it would add a difficulty overhead if it is implemented this way. However, I see that `pipenv` should keep the same behaviour while introducing more options as follows:

* `pipenv install --test-packages` this will install [packages] and [test-packages]
* `pipenv install` this will install [packages] and [dev-packages]

I do not think it will add more complexity to Pipfile.lock.

I'd love to contribute and do it myself, but I'd like to initiate a discussion like this before starting.

Thank you

Should we have some error handling in the XPath custom function to handle cases like if user provides bad parameters for the `has-class()` function? Here are some things I tried, just to see what happened. Passing no arguments like `sel.xpath('//div[has-class()]')` seems to be equivalent to `sel.xpath('//div[@class]')`. Is that expected? Passing empty string seems to bring only elements that contain more than one class (side-effect of the spaces trick): >>> sel.xpath('//div[has-class("")]') [\r\n '>] Not sure what to do about the behavior above... Maybe that's fine? Passing a XPath nodeset raises an error: ``` >>> sel = Selector(u'container
decoy
main content
') >>> sel.xpath('//div[has-class(//span/text())]') Traceback (most recent call last): File "", line 1, in File "parsel/selector.py", line 222, in xpath **kwargs) File "src/lxml/lxml.etree.pyx", line 1587, in lxml.etree._Element.xpath (src/lxml/lxml.etree.c:59836) File "src/lxml/xpath.pxi", line 307, in lxml.etree.XPathElementEvaluator.__call__ (src/lxml/lxml.etree.c:172696) File "src/lxml/xpath.pxi", line 223, in lxml.etree._XPathEvaluatorBase._handle_result (src/lxml/lxml.etree.c:171610) File "src/lxml/lxml.etree.pyx", line 324, in lxml.etree._ExceptionContext._raise_if_stored (src/lxml/lxml.etree.c:12167) File "src/lxml/extensions.pxi", line 843, in lxml.etree._extension_function_call (src/lxml/lxml.etree.c:168218) File "parsel/xpathfuncs.py", line 40, in has_class if ' ' + cls + ' ' not in node_cls: TypeError: cannot concatenate 'str' and 'list' objects ``` Maybe we could handle this cases as well? In this case I believe it makes sense to apply the same behavior XPath has when converting a nodeset to a string. In fact, if I convert it to string first, it works: ``` >>> sel.xpath('//div[has-class(string(//span/text()))]') [main content'>] ``` Also, the same TypeError from before happens if you try other XPath types ([see the spec for more ideas](https://www.w3.org/TR/xpath/#contents)): ``` >>> sel.xpath('//div[has-class(1)]') # numbers Traceback (most recent call last): File "", line 1, in File "parsel/selector.py", line 222, in xpath **kwargs) .. OMITTED FOR BREVITY File "parsel/xpathfuncs.py", line 40, in has_class if ' ' + cls + ' ' not in node_cls: TypeError: cannot concatenate 'str' and 'float' objects >>> sel.xpath('//div[has-class(1 and 2)]') # booleans Traceback (most recent call last): File "", line 1, in File "parsel/selector.py", line 222, in xpath **kwargs) .. OMITTED FOR BREVITY File "parsel/xpathfuncs.py", line 40, in has_class if ' ' + cls + ' ' not in node_cls: TypeError: cannot concatenate 'str' and 'bool' objects ``` Maybe we could raise a custom exception with a nicer error message? Possibly extending `etree.XPathError` which is [caught here](https://github.com/scrapy/parsel/blob/master/parsel/selector.py#L223).

Personally, I am not aware of any software that computes rotations with regard to X-axis (except for ``pyregion``). However, I am far from being familiar with all kind of software out there and so my proposal was based on observation of the behavior of DS9 as well as the way orientation angles are being computed in STScI software. [I *think* I have also found evidence of measuring angles from Y-axis in several other packages but this was so long ago that now I cannot recollect these packages.]

Based on what I know, I think making default rotation axis to be 2 (=Y-axis) would serve most people the best. When I did my original PR https://github.com/astropy/pyregion/pull/34 I set the default to 1 (X-axis) in order not to break existing applications using ``pyregion``. If most people think that having the default axis with regard to which angles are measured to be Y-axis - I am all for it.

@zpavlig you have to look into the PostgreSQL log to understand why is it not started. Most likely it finds out it cannot replay the WAL segment with max_connections set to the higher value than in the configuration (which has been recently fixed, see the changes above).

rm -rf /var/lib/pgsql/9.6/data*  is a sure way to eventually do this on the wrong node and wipe out your production cluster (google for the gillab database incident). Please, always use reinit.

> Btw, maybe an updated example of haproxy might help others.

Yeah, documentation is pretty outdated, we need to work on it. But actually we are open to any kind of collaboration, including documentation and providing nice configuration examples.

> If one sets synchronous_commit to on or remote_write, the master will reply to commits only after the slave receives the data as well (even if it was not written to disk yet, it is visible in postgres). 

**remote_write** only guaranties that transaction was written on disk on remote site to write ahead log (wal). It will take some time until replica will replay it. In postgresql-9.6 it would be possible to set `synchronous_commit` to **remote_apply**. It will do exactly what you want.
```
When set to remote_apply, commits will wait until replies from the current synchronous
standby(s) indicate they have received the commit record of the transaction and applied
it, so that it has become visible to queries on the standby(s).
```
 But don't forget, that synchronous commit **affects performance** of the master and setting it to **remote_apply** globally is not the best idea. I believe such behavior should be controlled by application (it's possible to change value of `synchronous_commit` at any time), because not every transaction should be applied immediately on replica.

> Oh and this requires synchronous_standby_names as well obviously.

Currently the only possible way to enable synchronous commit in Patroni is set `synchronous_standby_names` to **'*'** Sure, it's not that flexible as specifying list of standby names divided by comma, but it suits it's purpose. The name of synchronous standby is set as an `application_name` in primary_conninfo.

> For what you suggested as implementation, you want to add another parameter to primary_conninfo which allows one to specify to which server to connect? 

First of all it helps to identify node. And later one could use this names instead of **'*'**

1) `application_name` is **NOT set** in `primary_conninfo`
```
localhost/postgres=# select usename, application_name, client_addr, sync_state from pg_stat_replication;
  usename   │ application_name │ client_addr │ sync_state 
────────────┼──────────────────┼─────────────┼────────────
 replicator │ walreceiver      │ 127.0.0.1   │ sync
 replicator │ walreceiver      │ 127.0.0.1   │ potential
(2 rows)
```

2) `application_name` is **set** in `primary_conninfo`
```
localhost/postgres=# select usename, application_name, client_addr, sync_state from pg_stat_replication;
  usename   │ application_name │ client_addr │ sync_state 
────────────┼──────────────────┼─────────────┼────────────
 replicator │ postgresql2      │ 127.0.0.1   │ sync
 replicator │ postgresql0      │ 127.0.0.1   │ potential
(2 rows)
```
Basically `application_name` is matching with the node name (postgresql.name parameter from yml file).

@pierrehvz Is this issue still relevant for you?

Fun fact, what you suggest @guillett already exists in OpenFisca. The keywords are not `steps` and `level`, but `brackets` and `amount`, but it has been existing since 2014. (It is bugged right now, but the [fix is easy](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-core/compare/fix-amount-scale?expand=1)). 

Now, the fact is that no one uses it. Probably mainly because it's never been documented, but I can also see why it's not as convenient/useful as it seems to be.

If I take @LorenzoStacchioDev's example, and put all the logics in the formula, I'd get:

```py
age = person('age', period)
thresolds = parameters(period).taxes.IRPEF.income_not_taxable.threshold_retirement
return where(age <= 70, thresolds.under_70, thresolds.over_70)
```

with  a parameter:

```yaml
over_70:
  2017-01-01: 8000
under_70:
    2017-01-01: 7500
```

The formula is one line more than the first suggestion, reads pretty well, ans is unambiguous. 

If I make `threshold_retirement` a scale, the formula becomes:

```py
age = person('age', period)
thresolds = parameters(period).taxes.IRPEF.income_not_taxable.threshold_retirement
return thresolds.calc(age)
```

and I need to define a scale in the parameters:

```yaml
brackets:
  - amount:
        2017-01-01: 8000
    threshold:
       2017-01-01:  0
  - amount:
        2017-01-01: 7500
    threshold:
       2017-01-01:  70
```

I'm saving a few character in the formula, but
- The formula becomes less explicit. To understand what the formula is doing, I need to:
  - Figure out the nature of `threshold_retirement`
  - Understand the concept of an "fixed amount scale"
  - As @benjello said, there is an ambiguity about 70. The current implementation puts in in the 0-70 bracket, but that's far from obvious. And if I wanted 70 to be in the second bracket, that would not work.
- The parameter is more complex to define

Advantages:
    - This makes the body of the request much simpler, as we just need to define the scenario there.

Constraints
 
  - All the requested variables must be computed for the same period. This would be fine for mes-aides or embauche, may be a big limitation for other usages.
  - URL may be long to compute a lot of variables.

This makes sense, as the overhead of the chunks is quite small, even at 5MB the overhead in terms of bytes is on the order of 0.01%.  And because it is threaded, the overhead in terms of connection setup/teardown latency is masked by other threads actively working while new connections are being made.

One thing to consider is that larger split sizes mean more to retransmit in the event a part fails, so there could be a large down-side in exchange for a small up-side.

In one of our internal tools I set the split size based on the file size: 5MB (the minimum) if the file is less than 50GB, and the file size divided by 10,000 (the maximum number of parts) if it is over 50GB.  This has proven extremely robust and completely saturates our network connection.

I am thinking of making this a `@property` on the Request class.

And the plan is to calculate it by something like:

```python
# Resolve the resourcePath, enabling comparison to the path
resolved_resource_path = self.context['resourcePath']
for key in self.uri_params.keys():
    resolved_resource_path = resolved_resource_path.replace('{%s}' % key, self.uri_params[key])

# Take the path and discard the application resource path
base_path = self.context['path'][:self.context['path'].rfind(resolved_resource_path)]

# Get Scheme and Host from headers
scheme_and_host = '%s://%s' % (self.headers['x-forwarded-proto'], self.headers['host'])

# If using a non-standard port, append it to the scheme and host
if self.headers['x-forwarded-proto'].lower() == 'https' and self.headers['x-forwarded-port'] != 443:
    scheme_and_host += ':%s' % self.headers['x-forwarded-port']
    
if self.headers['x-forwarded-proto'].lower() == 'http' and self.headers['x-forwarded-port'] != 80:
    scheme_and_host += ':%s' % self.headers['x-forwarded-port']

# Return base path
return '%s/%s' % (scheme_and_host, base_path)
```


Some of this might seem a bit overkill, but here is my thinking:
- Setting the port is useful for `chalice local` which does not use the standard port
- Resolving the resource path and using that instead of stage is necessary for when using custom domains (and since they allow not setting a stage, which then becomes part of the url, we cannot simply assume that anything about the first / being magical)

We've had to do something similar because we see this error which never seems to recover (running in google container engine):

`grpc._channel._Rendezvous: <_Rendezvous of RPC that terminated with (StatusCode.UNAVAILABLE, OS Error)>`

I'm inclined to believe that a connection broke or shutdown, causing a socket to trigger an "OS Error". 
 This is not the only error we see, but since they show up sporadically it's hard to know which ones wedge us and which are properly handled.  For example DEADLINE_EXCEEDED is "normal" and processing goes on.

To work around the wedging, our code does something like this. We set a variable to indicate to the outer loop that the client might be bad.

```
class OurPolicy(thread.Policy):
    """                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               
    We occasionally see errors that google code doesn't recover from, so we                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           
    set a flag that let's the outer thread respond by restarting the client.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
    grpc._channel._Rendezvous: <_Rendezvous of RPC that terminated with (StatusCode.UNAVAILABLE, OS Error)>                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
    """
    _exception_caught = None

    def __init__(self, *args, **kws):
        logger.info(f'Initializing our PubSub Policy Wrapper')  # noqa                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                
        return super(OurPolicy, self).__init__(*args, **kws)

    def on_exception(self, exc):
        # If this is DEADLINE_EXCEEDED, then we want to retry by returning None instead of raise-ing                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  
        deadline_exceeded = grpc.StatusCode.DEADLINE_EXCEEDED
        code_value = getattr(exc, 'code', lambda: None)()
        logger.error(f'Caught Exception in PubSub Policy Wrapper: code={code_value} exc={exc}')
        if code_value == deadline_exceeded:
            return
        OurPolicy._exception_caught = exc
        # will just raise exc                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         
        return super(OurPolicy, self).on_exception(exc)


def get_publisher_client():
    return pubsub_v1.PublisherClient(
        batch_settings=types.BatchSettings(max_latency=0.001, max_messages=1))


def get_subscriber_client():
    return pubsub_v1.SubscriberClient(policy_class=OurPolicy)

```

then our listener can tell when we've hit an exception that would wedge our server, and instead it restarts the client:

```
class Worker(object):
   [..lots deleted..]

    def listen(self):
        logger.info('Listening, press Ctrl+C to exit.')
        subscriber = get_subscriber_client()
        subscription_path = subscriber.subscription_path(NOTIFY_PROJECT, self.subscription_name)
        while True:
            if not self.sub_client:
                logger.warning('Restarting pubsub subscriber client')
                self.sub_client = get_subscriber_client()
            self.sub_client.subscribe(subscription_path, callback=self.message_callback)
            try:
                # The subscriber is non-blocking, so we must keep the main thread from                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                
                # exiting to allow it to process messages in the background.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
                logger.info(f'Listening for messages on topic {self.topic_name} @ {subscription_path}')
                while True:
                    time.sleep(1)
                    if OurPolicy._exception_caught:
                        exc = OurPolicy._exception_caught
                        OurPolicy._exception_caught = None
                        raise exc
            except KeyboardInterrupt:
                logger.info('Stopped listening for tasks.')
                break
            except Exception as e:
                logger.exception(e)
                self.sub_client = None
                from raven.contrib.django.raven_compat.models import client
                client.captureException()

```

That said, I'm not sure if we should add some mutexes here and be more thread-safe.  Also this may have other issues since it's unclear if the old client ever shuts down cleanly.  There's nothing in the API docs about it, and python can be lazy about this kind of resource cleanup, especially since worker threads may linger with a reference to the old client object.

When starting from TRIM_HORIZON it's possible that it will take a number of GetRecords calls for the worker to catch up to the current tip of the shard.  For testing can you try:
1. Add `initialPositionInStream=LATEST` to the properties file.
2. Start the consumer process first, and wait for it to start reading from the stream.
    Once you see `Created new shardConsumer`.
3. Start the producing application.

This will ensure that the consumer is reading from the tip before you start putting data in.  This usually isn't a problem for application  that are running constantly as they stay right near the tip of the shard.

I was able to improve download speeds by 15-20% by making `requests` always return the biggest chunk it's downloaded currently, not in 10MB chunks. Next thing I'm trying, is to decrease the progress querying frequency to every 5 seconds. In the meantime, @lyw07 is looking into using more OS system calls to speed up copying of files.

However, I don't think we can get farther than that within the scope of 0.6. We're now bottlenecked at this point by Python's Global Interpreter Lock -- it can't copy bytes over to files if another thread is handling a request. GIL handling is improved in Python 3.2+, but it's not a serious upgrade over just running importcontent in another process.

If you want to present the same python API for two different facade versions, you're looking at a lowest-common-denominator API.  If a newer Juju supports the older facade, the simplest thing is to use the older facade version for all Jujus and ignore the newer one.

If you want the object-layer API for the newer facade to be a superset of the object-layer API for the older facade, you'll probably need a different class for each facade version, using inheritance to add additional capabilities and override some implementations.

As I mentioned previously, you're also going to run into issues connecting to the model once added due to needing to discharge a macaroon for the new model.  The [py-macaroon-bakery](https://github.com/go-macaroon-bakery/py-macaroon-bakery) should be what we need to add support for that, with [PR #2](https://github.com/go-macaroon-bakery/py-macaroon-bakery/pull/2) there possibly being sufficient to include that functionality in libjuju.

FWIW, the `test_sampler_template_error` test (which induces infinite recursion and waits for `RecursionError`) is failing due to a bug (or at least an unwanted behaviour) in Python3 `inspect` module, [`getfullargspec()`](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/master/Lib/inspect.py#L1082) function (called from [`getargspec()`](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/master/Lib/inspect.py#L1051), which is used in the `vartype_argument` decorator around `BinaryQuadraticModel.__init__()`).

Namely, when `getfullargspec()` tries to call a helper function `_signature_from_callable` ([here](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/master/Lib/inspect.py#L1116)), it catches the base `Exception`, recasting any error (including `RecursionError`) as `TypeError`. The standard (good) practice is to be as specific as possible when catching exceptions.

Note that `RecursionError` is somewhat of a misnomer, since it's *not necessarily caused by a recursion* -- but by a stack frame overflow -- which in this case happens when `_signature_from_callable()` is called.

So, due to all this, the test which expects `RecursionError`, gets `TypeError`, and fails.

We can abandon the feature this pull request brings (default values for constrained arguments with `@vartype_argument`), or we can refactor the base sampler that relies on a double (triple?) recursion that user has to break in his subclass. Or we can do both. :)

Default argument values are nice to have, but (of course) not critical. Regardless, it would probably be good to refactor that default infinite recursion.

@petrjasek I have considered using a single endpoint/resource, but I've chosen this option with 1 common endpoint + one per auth method for different reasons:

- minimal changes in backend, the most annoying one is the change of database endpoint (auth => auth_db), but beside that most of code is untouched
- we can have specific schema validation with different resource endpoints
- in client changes are also minimal, and we have easy way to call different auth (change endpoint and use specific API). For logout, we can still use the common endpoint (auth).
- for replacing auth method like LDAP, there is basically nothing to do (beside changing the module to import), and nothing in client neither. We can simulate this behaviour with a one API for all with some kind of default value though

globally a one endpoint for all is a good way, but it need to change a lot of things in code + tests, I'm not sure if it worth it. We can discuss it in the scrum.

Also there is a egg and chicken problem with merging: merge need to be done in backend to validate tests in client, but this will break all superdesk during the change. It's probably more secure to use a branch during the merge then move everything to master when tests are validated. An other point we can discuss in scrum.

@oberstet, I think you're reading too much into the specification (https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-oberstet-hybi-tavendo-wamp-02). The spec defines the network protocol, it does not define what is an appropriate to expose in an API. Subscription options are are a valid part of the network protocol and can provide "provide additional subscription request details in a extensible way". There is no reason not to include them in the Python API. They are exposed in the Autobahn JS API and we've found them incredibly useful mechanism to include parameters in topic subscriptions. The only alternative would be to embed topic parameters in the topic name itself. As it is, without having parity with Autobahn JS, the Python API will alienate a group of users who would otherwise find it incredibly useful.

I ran into this problem today.

Re: this errror:

```
parse error: Invalid literal at line 1, column 4
```

This is because of an error either in `debug-etcd`, or a change in the etcd API.

The `debug-etcd` service looks like this:

```
core@ip-10-128-1-31 ~ $ systemctl cat debug-etcd
# /etc/systemd/system/debug-etcd.service
[Unit]
Description=etcd debugging service

[Service]
ExecStartPre=/usr/bin/curl -sSL -o /opt/bin/jq http://stedolan.github.io/jq/download/linux64/jq
ExecStartPre=/usr/bin/chmod +x /opt/bin/jq
ExecStart=/usr/bin/bash -c "while true; do curl -sL http://127.0.0.1:4001/v2/stats/leader | /opt/bin/jq . ; sleep 1 ; done"
```

Notice that `curl` is piping into `jq`. It turns out that if you `curl -sL http://127.0.0.1:4001/v2/stats/leader` on a non-leader etcd peer, the response is `not current leader`, which `jq` fails to parse with the `invalid literal` error message.

As for the root problem of etcd not setting keys, I am also experiencing this issue on a new 1.7.3 cluster.

> Memory consumption grows because packets get buffered faster than removed from the buffer.

Presumably they're being buffered by pcapy or Python; libpcap doesn't buffer an arbitrary number of packets.  (This is Linux, so it's probably using TPACKET with a fixed-size buffer shared between the kernel and userland, with packets simply being discarded by the kernel if they're arriving faster than userland can consume them; even if it's an ancient kernel without TPACKET, so that libpcap is doing `recvfrom()` calls, it does them into a buffer that's allocated at open time - it doesn't allocate a new buffer for each packet.)

Due to the complexity and fragility of wrapping the htslib C API, there are many limitations in the pysam VariantHeader API.  Some will be resolved in time, but there are just too many ways to crash the Python interpreter when altering metadata.  (Fine in C, where one has to be aware of low level details regarding memory management, but hard to translate into a generic object model in Python.)

Regarding your specific problems:
  * The "PASS" filter is reserved by the htslib implementation and one should not remove or alter it.  I can work on adding the ability to mutate the description, but it is a purely cosmetic thing.
  * The call to remove the header just removes the header line, but one cannot remove the header metadata or else subsequently reading records with that header will cause a segmentation fault.

Here is the workaround on windows if you don't have any clusters configured yet:

```
$path = "$env:USERPROFILE\.kube\config"
az aks get-credentials --resource-group=CloudServices --name=CloudServicesKubernetesCluster --file - > $path
(Get-Content $path -Raw).Replace("`r`n","`n") | Set-Content $path -Force
```

Please note that it will replace existing kubectl configuration

FROM golang:1.8.3-stretch

### For golang testing stuff
RUN go get -u github.com/golang/lint/golint
RUN go get github.com/jstemmer/go-junit-report

### License parser
RUN go get github.com/coreos/license-bill-of-materials

### 'grafiti' for cluster cleanup
ENV GRAFITI_VERSION "v0.1.1"
RUN git clone -q https://github.com/coreos/grafiti.git ${GOPATH}/src/github.com/coreos/grafiti \
    && cd ${GOPATH}/src/github.com/coreos/grafiti \
    && git checkout -q tags/${GRAFITI_VERSION} \
    && make install

# /go needs to be writable by jenkins user like it is in the upstream golang image
RUN chmod 777 -R /go

### Install Shellcheck, Terraform, NodeJS and Yarn
ENV SHELLCHECK_VERSION="v0.4.6"
ENV TERRAFORM_VERSION="v0.9.11-coreos.2"
ENV NODE_VERSION="v8.1.2"
ENV YARN_VERSION="v0.24.6"
ENV MATCHBOXVERSION="v0.6.1"
# yarn needs a home writable by any user running the container
ENV HOME /opt/home
RUN mkdir -p ${HOME}
RUN chmod 777 -R ${HOME}

RUN apt-get update
RUN DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive apt-get install -y -q apt-utils
RUN DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive apt-get install --no-install-recommends -y -q \
    build-essential sudo curl wget git autoconf automake unzip libtool jq awscli gnupg1 \
    openvpn xvfb xauth openssl python-pip python-setuptools

# Install azure cli
RUN echo "deb [arch=amd64] https://packages.microsoft.com/repos/azure-cli/ wheezy main" | tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/azure-cli.list
RUN apt-key adv --keyserver packages.microsoft.com --recv-keys 417A0893
RUN DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive apt-get install -y -q apt-transport-https
RUN apt-get update && DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive apt-get install -y -q azure-cli

# Install Terraform
# TERRAFORM_URL enables us to build the upstream-terraform Tectonic builder
# image (See README.md/##Upstream-and-CoreOS-Terraform)
ARG TERRAFORM_URL=https://github.com/coreos/terraform/releases/download/${TERRAFORM_VERSION}/linux_amd64.zip
RUN curl -L ${TERRAFORM_URL} | funzip > /usr/local/bin/terraform && chmod +x /usr/local/bin/terraform

# Install NodeJS
RUN cd /tmp && \
    wget --quiet -O /tmp/node.tar.gz https://nodejs.org/dist/${NODE_VERSION}/node-${NODE_VERSION}-linux-x64.tar.gz && \
    tar xf node.tar.gz && \
    rm -f /tmp/node.tar.gz && \
    cd node-* && \
    cp -r lib/node_modules /usr/local/lib/node_modules && \
    cp bin/node /usr/local/bin && \
    ln -s /usr/local/lib/node_modules/npm/bin/npm-cli.js /usr/local/bin/npm
# so any container user can install global node modules if needed
RUN chmod 777 /usr/local/lib/node_modules

# Install Yarn
RUN cd /tmp && \
    wget --quiet -O /tmp/yarn.tar.gz https://github.com/yarnpkg/yarn/releases/download/${YARN_VERSION}/yarn-${YARN_VERSION}.tar.gz && \
    tar xf yarn.tar.gz && \
    rm -f /tmp/yarn.tar.gz && \
    mv /tmp/dist /usr/local/yarn && \
    ln -s /usr/local/yarn/bin/yarn /usr/local/bin/yarn

# Install Shellcheck
RUN cd /tmp && \
    wget --quiet https://storage.googleapis.com/shellcheck/shellcheck-${SHELLCHECK_VERSION}.linux.x86_64.tar.xz && \
    tar xJf shellcheck-${SHELLCHECK_VERSION}.linux.x86_64.tar.xz && \
    mv /tmp/shellcheck-${SHELLCHECK_VERSION}/shellcheck /usr/local/bin/shellcheck

# Install Chrome for installer gui tests
# Use Chrome beta because v60 or higher is needed for headless mode
RUN wget --quiet -O - https://dl-ssl.google.com/linux/linux_signing_key.pub | apt-key add - && \
    sh -c 'echo "deb [arch=amd64] http://dl.google.com/linux/chrome/deb/ stable main" >> /etc/apt/sources.list.d/google-chrome.list' && \
    apt-get update && \
    apt-get install --no-install-recommends -y -q \
    google-chrome-beta ca-certificates

# matchbox for baremetal installer gui tests
RUN cd /tmp && \
    wget --quiet -O /tmp/matchbox.tar.gz https://github.com/coreos/matchbox/releases/download/${MATCHBOXVERSION}/matchbox-${MATCHBOXVERSION}-linux-amd64.tar.gz && \
    tar xzvf /tmp/matchbox.tar.gz && \
    rm -f /tmp/matchbox.tar.gz && \
    cp matchbox-*/matchbox /usr/local/bin

# cleanup
RUN rm -rf /tmp/node-v*
RUN rm -f /tmp/yarn.tar.gz
```

I really needed this and was too lazy to figure out the exact mechanics of `defvjp()` or rather the signature of the functions it takes, to my solution was to directly copy the implementation of `logsumexp` which is pure python and differentiate it with autograd.

```
def lse(a, axis=None, b=None, keepdims=False, return_sign=False):
#    a = _asarray_validated(a, check_finite=False)
    if b is not None:
#        a, b = np.broadcast_arrays(a,b)
        if np.any(b == 0):
            a = a + 0.  # promote to at least float
            a[b == 0] = -np.inf

    a_max = np.amax(a, axis=axis, keepdims=True)

#    if a_max.ndim > 0:
#        a_max[~np.isfinite(a_max)] = 0
#    elif not np.isfinite(a_max):
#        a_max = 0

    if b is not None:
#        b = np.asarray(b)
        tmp = b * np.exp(a - a_max)
    else:
        tmp = np.exp(a - a_max)

    # suppress warnings about log of zero
    with np.errstate(divide='ignore'):
        s = np.sum(tmp, axis=axis, keepdims=keepdims)
        if return_sign:
            sgn = np.sign(s)
            s *= sgn  # /= makes more sense but we need zero -> zero
        out = np.log(s)

    if not keepdims:
        a_max = np.squeeze(a_max, axis=axis)
    out += a_max

    if return_sign:
        return out, sgn
    else:
        return out

grad_lse = ag.grad(lse)
```

Notice all of the stuff I commented out because it wasn't supported by autograd. This means there are a few assumptions such as `a` and `b` being the same shape.

You are correct that merging baskets will never remove items, but I would not say that adding items is "safe." If you add an item to a basket without the user expecting it, it's possible that they will accidentally purchase the item. This isn't usually an issue if you add two different items, because it's usually pretty "obvious" when a second line is added to the cart. However, the merging algorithm _adds_ line quantities together for identical items, so two items could become three.

The message I wrote is a little verbose, for sure, but I was trying to make it as clear as possible to a less savvy user. "We have merged your baskets" prompts me to ask "What baskets? I only had one basket" which is why I added the verbiage "from a previous session".

@kuba , I tried Homebrew's `pyenv-virtualenv` formula instead of `pip` + `virtualenv`, and here's what I observed:

* Once the `pyenv` and `pyenv-virtualenv` formulas are installed, they instruct the user to change their shell init scripts to [add initialization commands](https://github.com/yyuu/pyenv-virtualenv#installing-with-homebrew-for-os-x-users) to them. I don't know if it would be possible to (or even if we should) automate this configuration step on behalf of the user. 
* The `pyenv-virtualenv` command should be used in lieu of `virtualenv`. This would require changes in `dev/_venv_common.sh`, or that we instruct users to create an alias.

> As a Python-ignoramus, is it considered OK to move parameters around (e.g. `dataSource` on `Template`) ?

Generally, no. This was a backwards incompatible change and really should have been flagged as such in the changelog. Mea culpa.

Unfortunately, if you have a thing `f(x, y, z, foo=None, bar=None)` as above, and you want to make `y` optional, you _have_ to move it, because Python doesn't allow `f(x, y=None, z, foo=None, bar=None)`.

You could always make a new thing, `Templatev2`, but that presents a maintenance burden that I'm not ready to deal with yet, and is probably not appropriate for a pre-1.0 project.

What I meant by “django-cachalot handles subqueries” is simply that they are correctly taken into account when caching and invalidating data. This means a query containing any number of ultra-complex non-raw subqueries will still be cached, and it will correctly be invalidated if any of the tables from that query (and its subqueries) gets modified.

I see your point, I work on a similar project, [dezede/dezede](https://github.com/dezede/dezede), that also displays a ton of related data using a lot of `JOIN`s.

What I suggest you to do is to simply try using django-cachalot in development and test it against the most expensive queries. Pay attention to the RAM usage, it may be too big for your server.
Without django-debug-toolbar (it has a huge overhead on SQL queries), test the time spent on these pages with and without django-cachalot.

If the benefits are negligible, don’t use django-cachalot. Your best option will be to code optimisations and systems such as an infinite pagination.

In the future, please ask questions [on gitter](https://gitter.im/django-cachalot/Lobby).

@bmihelac In commit:dd1c0852bac0a499693a2b31a42457e2134d266c you removed the exception handling from the call to `before_import`.

Did you mean to remove the `raise()` as well as the rollback?

Because what happens now is that if you call `import_data` with `raise_errors=True` and `before_import` has an `Exception`, then it logs the exception but doesn't raise it. So the `Resource` then carries on and tries to import all the rows in the dataset, which isn't what we want. The `after_import` call does raise errors if requested.

I will prepare an MR to add

```
            if raise_errors:
                raise
```

to the `before_import` call.

angr doesn't know where the real main function inside the PE is located, thus is uses the PE entry point instead. In consequence, 'main' points to the function which initializes the runtime.

IDA is able to parse the OVERLAY section generated by the compiler, so the functions get renamed to their proper names instead of generic ones. Using the OVERLAY section the same way IDA does should allow to determining where 'main' starts.
Note that name information is normally not embedded in PEs (when compiled with VS).

One thing we discussed before (and I can't tell whether this PR addresses this) is that by default, `MassStorageFile` should be unconfigured and not work until the user has provided some default arguments. Likewise, it is likely that `SharedFile` should require some configuration before it can be used.

I feel that it's useful having DIRAC running in a separate environment so that we aren't, for exampe, tied to using the same Python version (e.g. they might require 2.6 while we have 2.7 or we might want to move to 3 but can't since DIRAC doesn't support it).

From the point of view of most of the Ganga code, there should be little (or no) difference between a persistent server and what we have now. Having a persistent server means another thing we have to keep track of, start correctly and shutdown afterwards. It potentially gives a performance improvement due to reduced overheads but unless we know that it is a bottleneck, it is probably not worth the investment of effort.

Batching some DIRAC calls together seems like a good idea, especially in some of the simpler cases. We shouldn't try to be too clever about it but this could help reduce the aforementioned overheads a tad.

The transaction locks at this stage are effectively get locks with the advantage that we can bail-out of read/write scenarios where we know we don't need to request a file lock on disk as we are already using the object in memory.

The slope in the Roofline model is the bandwidth limited region, where `I * b_s < P_{max}`, where `I` is the arithmetic intensity (plotted on the x axis) and `b_s` the applicable peak bandwidth (4th column). The prediction will yield the lowest performance (the bottleneck) by using the arithmetic intensity (also referred to as computational intensity), found in the second column.

You find multiple arithmetic intensities in the table, because in each cache level, as well as main memory, fewer accesses may appear. This depends on the access pattern and iteration order in the code. In many cases the main memory accesses will limit the performance, but in the 3d-27pt example with N=100 and M=500, you see that this is not always the case and the L3 cache bandwidth is the limiting bottleneck.

You can find some slides on the Roofline model at https://moodle.rrze.uni-erlangen.de/pluginfile.php/10660/mod_resource/content/1/04_Roofline_Jacobi.pdf

So the reason why I suggest doing this a different way is to maintain the original purpose of the __ name __ == " __ main __ " statement which is to separate the operation of a script from the input of a script, basically allowing a file to be a script and a module. This separation technique also prevents future name collisions if say one of us decides to change the name of kwargs or flags. Because one of these has to be favoured over the other if this were to happen instead of breaking and throwing a AttributeError so you could fix the problem this external script which is theoretically using the other script would grab from the other incorrect variable and continue working incorrectly making debugging more challenging and possibly creating further errors. Basically this proofs these scripts more powerfully against changes. A further benefit is that we can make this function that encapsulates command line parsing a standard name which means it can be understood by devs not to call this function as it is not meant to be used except by the top level. Also should make the whole entry point section in setup.py easier because every entry point will be the same.

> Is there anything specific that makes my exception class problematic (compared to the stdexcept classes for example), or is this is a generic issue that more people are likely to hit?

If I remember correctly, this isn't doesn't affect stdlibc++ (at least for non-ancient versions), but does affect libc++ (are you on macOS, by any chance?  Or on Linux using clang with libc++?).  But no, there isn't anything specific to your class: pybind has to deal with the same issue internally for exception class wrappers that we use by registering one exception handler for each module:
https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/blob/2d0507db43cd5a117f7843e053b17dffca114107/include/pybind11/detail/internals.h#L146-L160.

Somehow, there is a weird chain of dependencies and consequences there, isn't it? Because it is not copy-constructible, it will be a "move-only holder", but because there is no move constructor, this semi-copy-contructor `std::auto_ptr(std::auto_ptr&)` is called?
Related to that, what if you would have a smart pointer that can do two thing? It can either move the ownership (`SmartPtr<T>(SmartPtr<T>&&)`), or it can make a (deep) copy of the pointee object (`SmartPtr>T>(const SmartPtr<T>&`). In the current solution in your branch, pybind11 would prefer the copy-ctr instead of the move-ctr, right? That somehow feels inconsistent with C++'s default way of preferring a move over a copy, and using the copy-ctr as a fall-back option for rvalue references.

> When I try to run a dry run of my certificate renewal process, I understand that since apache2 is listening on port 80, I have to stop it.

This is only true if you're using the `standalone` plugin. If you're using the `apache` or `webroot` plugin, Apache can (and in `webroot`'s case should) continue to run. You can find more information about the different plugins available [here](https://certbot.eff.org/docs/using.html#plugins).

> However, when I do stop it, as soon as certbot "installs" the first certificate, apache2 is started automatically. 

After installing your certificate, Certbot does a graceful reload of Apache. This is done because if Apache is currently running, it must be reloaded for the new certificate to take effect. We could potentially look into adding logic to skip this step if Apache isn't currently running, but this might be tricky to do reliably across all systems.

In the absence of such a feature, I recommend seeing if you can use the `webroot` or `apache` plugins during renewal which will avoid this problem entirely. This has the added benefit that you don't have to stop/start Apache to renew your certs. You'll simply run Certbot, all your certificates will be renewed, and the certs you've set up to install with Apache will be updated and served by the webserver when Certbot exits.

To change the plugins used to renew your certificate, it's probably easiest to renew once with `certonly` as described in our [renewal documentation](https://certbot.eff.org/docs/using.html#renewal). Alternatively, you can modify the files found in `/etc/letsencrypt/renewal`, however, this is easy to mess up, especially if switching to something like `webroot` which will require more information in order to work.

You need to kill or wait for other Certbot processes running on your machine to finish. I suspect if you try running `letsencrypt-auto` again, you won't have this problem, but if you do, run `pgrep letsencrypt,certbot` to get the process ID of the running instances or `pkill letsencrypt,certbot` to just kill them.

I'm not @ohemorange, but I think the goal of #4319 is to ensure that operations involving `os.path.exists` work as expected even when other operations are happening concurrently.

Consider this excerpt from `storage.py`:

```135) def rename_renewal_config(prev_name, new_name, cli_config):
136)     """Renames cli_config.certname's config to cli_config.new_certname.
137) 
138)     :param .NamespaceConfig cli_config: parsed command line
139)         arguments
140)     """
141)     prev_filename = renewal_filename_for_lineagename(cli_config, prev_name)
142)     new_filename = renewal_filename_for_lineagename(cli_config, new_name)
143)     if os.path.exists(new_filename):
144)         raise errors.ConfigurationError("The new certificate name "
145)             "is already in use.")
146)     try:
147)         os.rename(prev_filename, new_filename)
148)     except OSError:
149)         raise errors.ConfigurationError("Please specify a valid filename "
150)             "for the new certificate name.")
```

Currently, we check whether the `new_filename` is in use on line 143 before renaming a file to that name on line 147. However, some concurrent operation could create a file with `new_filename` between when `os.path.exists` is evaluated on line 143 and when `os.rename` is evaluated on line 147, causing us to overwrite a file ― the very thing we're trying to protect against on line 143.

On newer versions of Python, I think `os.replace` might be helpful here. However, I'm not sure what the best practice would be for the set of versions we support.

Hi @colobas, scheduling works a bit differently in Ray. Each machine has its own scheduler (which is responsible for managing the workers on that machine), and failures are handled at the granularity of machines, so if the scheduler on a given machine dies, the whole machine is considered to have failed. Objects that were lost and are needed will be recreated by rerunning the tasks that created those objects (specifications of the tasks are stored in a sharded in-memory database, currently we are not resilient to the failure of this database, but we're prototyping a fault tolerance scheme based on chain replication for it).

Currently there are some limitations of the kinds of failures we handle as described in http://ray.readthedocs.io/en/latest/fault-tolerance.html.

One place that *could* be rewritten with asyncio is our worker processes in `worker.py`. In the current implementation, there are two threads.

1. The main thread runs an infinite loop where it gets a task from the local scheduler and executes the task.
2. A background thread has a Redis pubsub client which listens for new remote function definitions from Redis and fetches/unpickles them.

There is a small amount of coordination between the two threads using a lock.

Conceptually, this would probably be cleaner with Python 3's support for event loops.

@RosadinTV said: 

> @beardypig 
> Maybe is a good idea to add -v=1 parameter by default? 
> (Because this one Works well in both builds)

**Not at all**; making `-v=1` the default will mean ALL RTMP streams 
will be fetched as if they were LIVE, i.e. IN REAL TIME; this setting will, 
among other things, break user experience when the -o switch is deployed 
with VOD streams; by default, rtmpdump downloads non-live streams 
at full speeds (bandwidth limited), so a 1hr stream can be fetched in 10mins; 
making -v the default would mean the same stream would need 1 full hour 
to be dumped on disk :cry:  
I, for one, am against it - feel free to add some info on the -R=1 (for VOD) 
switch in the documentation; and **REALLY LIVE** RTMP streams do not need 
the -v switch by default anyway (only in rare occasions where the stream is 
misconfigured on the FMS server and rtmpdump fails to identify it correctly!)

I think this is out of scope for Streamlink in general. There is already support for a Playlist of streams, but it is an API available to plugins and not intended to be used to string together arbitrary streams - this would be fraught with issues. 

It is entirely possible to add support for a Playlist of streams on plugin by plugin basis, eg. the crunchyroll plugin could offer some method to specify a list of episodes to play... 

Of course, if you are using Linux with bash (or similar) you can do something like this
```sh
for u in http://www.crunchyroll.com/ninja-girl-samurai-master/episode-1-lets-go-see-nobunaga-sama-721031 http://www.crunchyroll.com/ninja-girl-samurai-master/episode-2-the-oda-clan-and-their-merry-friends-721033; do
    streamlink -O "${u}" best; 
done | vlc -
```

Or put the URLs in a file

**episodes.txt**
```
http://www.crunchyroll.com/ninja-girl-samurai-master/episode-1-lets-go-see-nobunaga-sama-721031
http://www.crunchyroll.com/ninja-girl-samurai-master/episode-2-the-oda-clan-and-their-merry-friends-721033
```

and do 
```sh
for u in $(cat episodes.txt); do
    streamlink -O "${u}" best; 
done | vlc -
```

This is copied from a gitter chat message I made earlier:
It looks like the bad taxon ids are coming from PAINT. I downloaded the paint ecocyc file we have in the release and it has a bunch of NCBITaxon: entries in it. https://github.com/geneontology/helpdesk/issues/141#issuecomment-403171027
Investigating how the ids didn’t get switched is a little bit of a journey. So we internally use NCBITaxon:nnn has our taxon id. So when the gaf is being read we do a simple string replace of taxon -> NCBITaxon. So far so good.
Then in the writer, we’re prepared to accept valid ids of the form taxon:nnnn or NCBITaxon:nnnn. If the id is the internal representation we switch to the external taxon and write it out. Otherwise (if the id is already taxon:nnnn - and this is checked) then we keep it. The catch is we merely return the given ID if we don’t match either case. Since NCBITaxon: does not match either regex, we just pass it through the writer and the gaf reader is also not fully validating the ID.
I propose we push upstream to PAINT to let them know something might be wonky with their ids. We also need some ontobio changes to fix taxon id parsing. This should be caught by the parser at first. The Writer expects the data has already been cleaned. So this will be gaf parser work.

Just to be clear, this is fixed in the upstream data, so there is no longer bad data. We do still need to fix this in ontobio.

Good catch. I don't think that threejs has been migrated to ipywidgets 7.0.0. As far as I can tell, the migration would involve:
 - setting the `_model_module_version` traitlet explicitly -- at the moment it defaults to the version on the base widget (hence why it tries to look for jupyter-threejs@0.3.0 on unpkg).
 - changing references from `jupyter-js-widgets` to `@jupyter-widgets/base`.

We should probably write a migration guide for widget libraries and make an effort to help widget library developers with the migration?

That said, looking at ipyleaflet 0.4.0-alpha1, which *has* been migrated, the htmlmanager still doesn't work. As far as I can tell, this is because we've split the htmlmanager from the base package. Before, we could just rely on declaring that `jupyter-js-widgets` is available as an external dependency when building external widgets. Now (looking at webpack.config.js for ipyleaflet and the cookiecutter), we declare that `@jupyter-widgets/base` should be provided as an external dependency, but we don't provide it explicitly.

The most obvious fix that comes to mind, unless I'm missing something, is to not declare that `@jupyter-widgets/base` should be provided externally in the embed bundle of ipyleaflet. The base widget classes would then get bundled with ipyleaflet.
